title: Creating Your First Program
content:
# Creating Your First Program

Welcome to Modus! Creating a program for your patient is the foundation of effective rehabilitation.

## Getting Started

To create a new program:

1. **Navigate to Programs** - Tap the Programs tab in the main navigation
2. **Tap the + button** - Located in the top-right corner
3. **Select a patient** - Choose who this program is for
4. **Choose a protocol** - Select from evidence-based templates (ACL, rotator cuff, etc.)

## Program Structure

Every program is built from **phases**:

- **Phase 1**: Initial healing and protection
- **Phase 2**: Restore range of motion and basic strength
- **Phase 3**: Progressive loading and functional training
- **Phase 4**: Return to sport/activity

Each phase contains multiple **sessions** (workouts) that progress over weeks.

## Best Practices

- Start with a proven protocol template rather than building from scratch
- Keep phase names clear and descriptive
- Set realistic duration for each phase (typically 2-6 weeks)
- Add detailed notes for patient guidance

## Tips

💡 **Pro tip**: Name your programs based on the injury and patient (e.g., "John ACL Rehab 2024")

💡 **Remember**: You can always edit and adjust programs as your patient progresses

title: Assigning Programs to Patients
content:
# Assigning Programs to Patients

Once you've created a program, it's time to assign it to your patient so they can start their rehabilitation journey.

## Assignment Process

1. **From Program Builder**: When creating a new program, select the patient from the dropdown
2. **From Patient Profile**: Navigate to the patient's profile and tap "Assign Program"
3. **Automatic Activation**: Programs activate immediately upon assignment

## What Happens After Assignment?

When you assign a program:

- The patient sees the program in their **Today** tab
- They receive their first scheduled workout
- Progress tracking begins automatically
- You can monitor their compliance in the Analytics view

## Managing Multiple Programs

Patients can have multiple programs, but only one can be **active** at a time:

- Active programs appear in the patient's Today view
- Inactive programs are archived but viewable
- You can switch active programs from the patient profile

## Important Notes

⚠️ **Starting dates**: Programs start on the day they're assigned. Plan accordingly!

⚠️ **Program updates**: Changes to the program template don't affect already-completed sessions

## Best Practices

- Review the program with your patient before assignment
- Ensure they understand the progression timeline
- Set clear expectations for workout frequency
- Schedule a follow-up to check initial progress

title: Starting a Workout
content:
# Starting a Workout

Ready to train? Here's how patients start and complete their workout sessions.

## Finding Today's Workout

1. **Open the app** and navigate to the **Today** tab
2. **Review the session** - See all prescribed exercises
3. **Tap "Start Session"** - Begin your workout

## During Your Workout

As you complete each exercise:

### Logging Sets
- Tap on each exercise to expand it
- Enter the **weight/load** used
- Record **reps completed** for each set
- Add check marks as you finish sets

### Rating Your Experience
- **RPE (Rate of Perceived Exertion)**: How hard did it feel? (1-10 scale)
- **Pain Level**: Any discomfort during the exercise? (0-10 scale)
- **Notes**: Add comments about form, difficulty, or modifications

## Completing Your Session

1. **Finish all exercises** - Check off every set
2. **Review your work** - Ensure all data is logged
3. **Tap "Complete Session"** - Finalize and save
4. **Session summary** appears showing your total volume and effort

## Tips for Success

💪 **Be honest with RPE and pain ratings** - This helps your therapist adjust your program

💪 **Log in real-time** - Don't wait until after the workout when details are fuzzy

💪 **Use rest timers** - The app will guide you on rest periods between sets

## What If I Can't Finish?

No problem! You can:
- Save partial progress and return later
- Mark sets as skipped with a reason
- Leave notes for your therapist explaining any issues

title: Understanding Phases
content:
# Understanding Phases

Rehabilitation programs are divided into **phases** - distinct periods focused on specific goals as you recover.

## Why Phases Matter

Phases ensure:
- **Safe progression** from simple to complex movements
- **Tissue healing** follows biological timelines
- **Skill development** builds systematically
- **Return to activity** happens when you're truly ready

## Common Phase Structure

### Phase 1: Protection & Early Motion (Weeks 1-2)
- **Goal**: Reduce pain and swelling
- **Focus**: Gentle range of motion, isometrics
- **Intensity**: Very low, controlled movements

### Phase 2: Strength Foundation (Weeks 3-6)
- **Goal**: Restore full range of motion, build base strength
- **Focus**: Progressive resistance, stability work
- **Intensity**: Moderate, gradually increasing

### Phase 3: Progressive Loading (Weeks 7-12)
- **Goal**: Build functional strength and power
- **Focus**: Sport-specific movements, higher loads
- **Intensity**: High, approaching pre-injury levels

### Phase 4: Return to Activity (Weeks 13+)
- **Goal**: Safe return to full activity
- **Focus**: Performance testing, sport simulation
- **Intensity**: Maximum, competition-ready

## Phase Advancement

You progress to the next phase when you:
- ✅ Meet all strength and mobility criteria
- ✅ Complete the prescribed duration
- ✅ Pass functional tests (if applicable)
- ✅ Get clearance from your therapist

## Important Notes

⏱️ **Timelines are guidelines** - Your body may need more or less time

⏱️ **Don't rush** - Skipping phases increases re-injury risk

⏱️ **Communicate** - Tell your therapist if exercises feel too easy or hard

title: Adding Exercises
content:
# Adding Exercises

Learn how to add and customize exercises within your program phases.

## Exercise Templates

Modus includes a library of **exercise templates**:
- Pre-loaded with common rehabilitation exercises
- Organized by body region and movement pattern
- Include form cues and video demonstrations
- Searchable by name or category

## Adding an Exercise to a Session

1. **Open the session** you want to edit
2. **Tap "Add Exercise"** at the bottom
3. **Search or browse** the exercise library
4. **Select the exercise** template
5. **Prescribe parameters**:
   - Sets (how many times to repeat)
   - Reps (repetitions per set)
   - Load (weight or resistance)
   - Rest period (seconds between sets)

## Customizing Exercise Details

### Load Prescription
- **Percentage of max**: "75% of 1RM"
- **Specific weight**: "135 lbs"
- **Bodyweight**: No equipment needed
- **RPE-based**: "RPE 7-8"

### Rep Ranges
- **Fixed**: "10 reps" (exactly 10)
- **Range**: "8-12 reps" (allows variability)
- **Time-based**: "30 seconds" (for holds/planks)
- **To failure**: "AMRAP" (as many reps as possible)

## Exercise Order Matters

Arrange exercises strategically:

1. **Warm-up/mobility** - Prepare the body
2. **Main strength** - Heaviest, most demanding exercises
3. **Accessory work** - Supporting movements
4. **Cool-down** - Stretching and recovery

## Best Practices

✏️ **Include detailed notes** - Explain modifications or coaching cues

✏️ **Set realistic loads** - Start conservative, increase gradually

✏️ **Balance movement patterns** - Don't overload one area

✏️ **Consider equipment** - Ensure patients have access to needed tools

title: Progressive Overload Explained
content:
# Progressive Overload Explained

**Progressive overload** is the foundation of strength and rehabilitation gains. It means gradually increasing the demands on your body.

## The Principle

Your body adapts to stress by getting stronger. To keep improving:
- **Apply stress** (exercise)
- **Recover** (rest and nutrition)
- **Increase stress** (progressive overload)
- **Adapt** (get stronger)

## Methods of Progression

You can increase difficulty in several ways:

### 1. Load (Weight)
The most common method:
- Week 1: Squat 100 lbs × 3 sets × 10 reps
- Week 2: Squat 105 lbs × 3 sets × 10 reps
- Week 3: Squat 110 lbs × 3 sets × 10 reps

### 2. Volume (Sets × Reps)
Increase total work:
- Week 1: 3 sets × 8 reps
- Week 2: 3 sets × 10 reps
- Week 3: 4 sets × 10 reps

### 3. Frequency
Train more often:
- Month 1: Train 2× per week
- Month 2: Train 3× per week

### 4. Complexity
Advance movement difficulty:
- Week 1: Bodyweight squat
- Week 2: Goblet squat
- Week 3: Barbell back squat

### 5. Tempo
Control speed:
- Week 1: Normal tempo
- Week 2: 3-second eccentric (lowering)
- Week 3: Pause at bottom

## Tracking Progress in Modus

The app helps you apply progressive overload:
- **Session history** - See what you did last time
- **Auto-suggestions** - Get recommended increases
- **Progress charts** - Visualize strength gains over time
- **Volume tracking** - Monitor total workload

## Safe Progression Guidelines

📈 **The 10% Rule**: Don't increase weekly volume by more than 10%

📈 **Focus on one variable**: Change load OR volume, not both at once

📈 **Earn your progression**: Only increase when current level feels manageable (RPE 7-8)

📈 **Deload regularly**: Every 4-6 weeks, take a lighter week to recover

title: Logging Sets and Reps
content:
# Logging Sets and Reps

Accurate logging is crucial for tracking progress and informing program adjustments.

## Why Logging Matters

✅ **Tracks progress** - See strength gains over weeks
✅ **Informs adjustments** - Helps your therapist modify your program
✅ **Motivates** - Visual proof of your hard work
✅ **Prevents plateaus** - Ensures consistent progression

## How to Log a Set

1. **Complete the set** - Finish your repetitions
2. **Tap the set row** - Opens the logging interface
3. **Enter actual reps** - What you actually completed
4. **Enter load used** - Weight, resistance, or bodyweight
5. **Rate RPE** - How hard it felt (1-10)
6. **Rate pain** - Any discomfort (0-10)
7. **Tap "Save"** - Records the data

## What to Log

### Reps Completed
- **If prescribed 10 reps**: Log 10 (or less if you couldn't complete)
- **If prescribed 8-12 reps**: Log actual number (e.g., 11)
- **If to failure**: Log total reps achieved

### Load Used
- **Exact weight**: "135 lbs" for barbell, "25 lbs" for dumbbell
- **Bodyweight**: Log "BW" or "0" if using body only
- **Resistance bands**: Color or resistance level
- **Assisted**: Note assistance (e.g., "BW - 20 lbs assist")

### RPE (Rate of Perceived Exertion)
- **1-3**: Very easy, could do many more reps
- **4-6**: Moderate, could do several more reps
- **7-8**: Hard, 2-3 reps left in tank (ideal training zone)
- **9**: Very hard, 1 rep left
- **10**: Maximum effort, nothing left

### Pain Level
- **0**: No pain
- **1-3**: Mild discomfort, doesn't affect performance
- **4-6**: Noticeable pain, affects form
- **7-10**: Significant pain, stop exercise and notify therapist

## Best Practices

📝 **Log immediately** - Record right after each set

📝 **Be accurate** - Don't round up or embellish

📝 **Note modifications** - If you changed the exercise, explain why

📝 **Use the notes field** - Add context ("felt strong today" or "shoulder tight")

## Common Mistakes to Avoid

❌ Logging from memory after the workout
❌ Always hitting the prescribed numbers exactly
❌ Ignoring pain ratings
❌ Not noting when you modify an exercise

title: Rating Pain and RPE
content:
# Rating Pain and RPE

Understanding how to rate your pain and effort helps optimize your rehabilitation and prevents setbacks.

## RPE: Rate of Perceived Exertion

**RPE** measures how hard an exercise feels, regardless of the actual weight used.

### The RPE Scale (1-10)

- **RPE 1-2**: Minimal effort, like a warm-up
- **RPE 3-4**: Light effort, easy breathing
- **RPE 5-6**: Moderate, getting challenging
- **RPE 7**: Hard, could do 3 more reps
- **RPE 8**: Very hard, could do 2 more reps
- **RPE 9**: Extremely hard, maybe 1 more rep
- **RPE 10**: Maximum, absolute failure

### Ideal Training Zones

- **Rehabilitation (early)**: RPE 4-6
- **Strength building**: RPE 7-8
- **Testing maximal strength**: RPE 9-10

### Why RPE Matters

RPE adjusts for daily variations:
- Tired? Higher RPE at same weight
- Well-rested? Lower RPE, room to push
- Recovering from injury? RPE guides safe intensity

## Pain Rating Scale (0-10)

**Pain ratings** help distinguish good stress from harmful pain.

### Understanding the Scale

- **0**: No pain at all
- **1-2**: Minimal, barely noticeable
- **3-4**: Mild, aware but not bothersome
- **5-6**: Moderate, affects concentration
- **7-8**: Severe, difficult to continue
- **9-10**: Extreme, cannot continue

### Acceptable Pain Levels

✅ **0-3**: Safe zone - Continue as prescribed
⚠️ **4-5**: Caution zone - Monitor closely, may need modification
❌ **6+**: Stop zone - Cease exercise, notify therapist

## Types of Pain

### "Good" Discomfort
- Muscle burn during high reps
- Post-workout soreness (DOMS)
- Stretching sensation at end range
- Muscle fatigue

### Warning Signs (Bad Pain)
- Sharp, stabbing sensations
- Joint pain (not muscle)
- Pain that worsens during the set
- Pain that persists after rest
- Numbness or tingling

## When to Stop and Report

Immediately stop and contact your therapist if:
- Pain exceeds 5/10 during exercise
- Pain doesn't decrease with rest
- Swelling or redness appears
- Range of motion suddenly decreases
- You feel instability or "giving way"

## Using Ratings to Progress

Your ratings guide progression:
- **RPE 6 or less + pain 0-2**: Ready to increase load
- **RPE 7-8 + pain 0-2**: Perfect training intensity
- **RPE 9-10**: Consider reducing load
- **Pain 3+**: Modify exercise or reduce load

## Tips for Accurate Ratings

🎯 **Rate each set** - RPE changes as you fatigue

🎯 **Be honest** - Ratings guide your program, not a competition

🎯 **Consider the context** - "Good pain" vs concerning pain

🎯 **Use your therapist** - Discuss unclear situations

title: Completing a Session
content:
# Completing a Session

Properly finishing your workout ensures all data is saved and progress is tracked accurately.

## Finish Your Exercises

Before completing:

✅ **All exercises logged** - Every set has data
✅ **RPE and pain recorded** - For each set
✅ **Notes added** - Any relevant observations
✅ **Review your work** - Scan for missing entries

## The Completion Process

1. **Tap "Review Session"** - See summary of all exercises
2. **Check for missing data** - App highlights incomplete entries
3. **Add session notes** (optional):
   - How you felt overall
   - Any issues or concerns
   - Equipment substitutions
   - Energy levels
4. **Tap "Complete Session"** - Finalize and save

## What Gets Calculated

When you complete a session, Modus calculates:

### Total Volume
Sets × Reps × Load for all exercises
- Tracks your total workload
- Helps monitor training stress
- Guides progressive overload

### Average RPE
Mean effort across all sets
- Indicates session difficulty
- Helps gauge recovery needs

### Average Pain
Mean pain rating across all sets
- Monitors injury status
- Alerts therapist to issues

### Session Duration
Time from start to completion
- Tracks efficiency
- Monitors fatigue impact

## Session Summary

After completion, you'll see:
- **Total volume lifted** (lbs or kg)
- **Time spent** (minutes)
- **Average effort** (RPE)
- **Pain rating** (if any)
- **Exercises completed** (count)
- **Personal records** (if achieved)

## What Happens Next?

### Immediate Effects
- ✅ Session marked complete in your history
- ✅ Data synced to your therapist
- ✅ Progress charts updated
- ✅ Next session unlocked (if applicable)

### Therapist Notification
Your therapist can see:
- When you completed the workout
- Your effort and pain levels
- Any concerning trends
- Notes you added

## Can You Edit After Completion?

Yes! You can:
- View completed sessions in History
- Edit individual set data
- Add or modify notes
- Correct mistakes

Changes sync automatically to your therapist.

## Best Practices

📋 **Complete on the same day** - Don't delay, data stays fresh

📋 **Add meaningful notes** - Help your therapist help you

📋 **Review before finalizing** - Catch errors while fresh

📋 **Celebrate PRs** - The app highlights personal records!

## Incomplete Sessions

What if you can't finish?

- **Save progress** - Data is auto-saved as you go
- **Mark as partial** - Note what you couldn't complete
- **Explain why** - Add notes (injury, time, equipment)
- **Don't stress** - One incomplete session won't derail progress

title: Understanding Your Progress
content:
# Understanding Your Progress

Modus provides powerful analytics to visualize your rehabilitation journey and celebrate improvements.

## The Analytics Dashboard

Access progress charts from the **Analytics** tab:

### Strength Progress
- **Line graphs** showing load increases over time
- **Per exercise** - Track individual movements
- **Trend lines** - See overall trajectory
- **Week-over-week** - Compare recent sessions

### Volume Trends
- **Total volume** (sets × reps × load) per session
- **Weekly aggregates** - See training stress
- **Volume phases** - Match program progression

### Consistency Score
- **Sessions completed** vs scheduled
- **Adherence percentage** - Your compliance rate
- **Streak tracking** - Consecutive completed sessions

### Pain & RPE Trends
- **Average pain** over time (should decrease)
- **Average RPE** over time (may increase as you get stronger)
- **Correlation analysis** - Pain vs load relationships

## Reading Your Charts

### Strength Chart
- **X-axis**: Date or session number
- **Y-axis**: Load (weight) used
- **Multiple lines**: Different exercises
- **Dots**: Individual data points (sets)

**What to look for:**
- 📈 **Upward trend**: You're getting stronger!
- ➡️ **Plateau**: May need program adjustment
- 📉 **Downward**: Could indicate overtraining or injury

### Volume Chart
- **Bars**: Total volume per session
- **Color coding**: By exercise or phase
- **Height**: More volume = more work

**What to look for:**
- **Gradual increases**: Healthy progression
- **Sudden spikes**: May indicate too rapid advancement
- **Drops**: Deload weeks or missed exercises

## Progress Milestones

The app highlights key achievements:

🏆 **Personal Records (PRs)**
- Highest weight for an exercise
- Most reps at a given weight
- Highest total volume session

🎯 **Goal Achievements**
- Phase completion
- Return to baseline strength
- Functional test milestones

📅 **Streak Milestones**
- 7-day streak
- 30-day streak
- 90-day streak

## Interpreting Trends

### Positive Signs
- ✅ Steady increase in load
- ✅ Decreasing pain ratings
- ✅ Consistent RPE (7-8) despite higher loads
- ✅ High compliance (80%+ sessions completed)

### Warning Signs
- ⚠️ Increasing pain over time
- ⚠️ RPE increasing despite same loads
- ⚠️ Frequent missed sessions
- ⚠️ Declining volume

## Sharing Progress

Share your charts with:
- **Your therapist** - Built-in sharing in sessions
- **Healthcare team** - Export as PDF
- **Insurance** - Document medical necessity
- **Yourself** - Progress screenshots for motivation

## Using Data to Adjust

Your therapist uses your data to:
- Advance you to the next phase
- Modify exercise selection
- Adjust load prescriptions
- Identify exercises causing pain
- Celebrate your success!

## Tips for Better Analytics

📊 **Log consistently** - More data = better insights

📊 **Be accurate** - Honest logging reveals true trends

📊 **Check weekly** - Regular review keeps you motivated

📊 **Compare phases** - See how far you've come

title: Personal Records (PRs)
content:
# Personal Records (PRs)

**Personal Records** are your best performances - they represent your peak strength and progress milestones.

## What Counts as a PR?

Modus tracks several PR types:

### 1. Max Weight PR
- **Heaviest load** lifted for an exercise
- Regardless of reps (within reason)
- Example: Bench Press 135 lbs × 8 reps

### 2. Volume PR
- **Most total volume** in a single set
- Calculated: Weight × Reps
- Example: Squat 100 lbs × 15 reps = 1,500 lbs volume

### 3. Rep PR
- **Most reps** at a specific weight
- Example: 12 reps at 95 lbs (previous best was 10)

### 4. Session Volume PR
- **Highest total volume** in one workout
- Sum of all sets across all exercises
- Example: 15,000 lbs total session volume

## How PRs are Detected

The app automatically identifies PRs:

1. **During workout** - Compares to historical data
2. **At completion** - Highlights new records
3. **In analytics** - Tags PR data points
4. **Notifications** - Celebrates your achievement

## PR Strategy in Rehab

### Early Phase (1-6 weeks)
- **Expect frequent PRs** - You're regaining lost strength
- **Focus on form** - Don't chase PRs at expense of technique
- **Celebrate milestones** - Each PR shows recovery progress

### Middle Phase (7-12 weeks)
- **PRs slow down** - This is normal and healthy
- **Quality over quantity** - Fewer but more meaningful PRs
- **Look for rep PRs** - Same weight, more reps = strength

### Late Phase (13+ weeks)
- **PRs are rare** - You're approaching peak capacity
- **Maintenance PRs** - Holding strength is a victory
- **Functional PRs** - Focus on movement quality, not just load

## PR Etiquette

### Do:
- ✅ **Celebrate** - You earned it!
- ✅ **Share** - Tell your therapist
- ✅ **Document** - Take notes on how you felt
- ✅ **Use as motivation** - Proof of progress

### Don't:
- ❌ **Chase PRs daily** - You'll burn out
- ❌ **Ignore pain for PRs** - Never worth injury
- ❌ **Compare to others** - Your journey is unique
- ❌ **Judge bad days** - Not every session will be a PR

## Reading PR Indicators

In the app, PRs are marked with:
- 🏆 **Trophy icon** - Next to the set
- **Bold text** - In session summary
- **Highlight color** - On charts and graphs
- **Achievement badge** - In your profile

## PR Types by Exercise

Different exercises have different PR priorities:

### Compound Movements (Squat, Deadlift, Press)
- Focus on **max weight PRs**
- These show overall strength gains

### Accessory Exercises (Curls, Raises, etc.)
- Focus on **rep PRs** and **volume PRs**
- Load less important than quality reps

### Bodyweight Exercises (Push-ups, Pull-ups)
- Focus on **rep PRs**
- Added weight creates new PR categories

## PR Cycles

Expect PRs to come in cycles:

1. **Build phase** - Frequent PRs as you gain strength
2. **Plateau** - Few PRs, consolidating gains
3. **Deload** - No PRs expected (recovery week)
4. **Breakthrough** - New PRs after recovery

## Historical PRs

The app tracks:
- **All-time PRs** - Your absolute best
- **Phase PRs** - Best within current training phase
- **Monthly PRs** - Recent achievements
- **Pre-injury PRs** - Goals for return to baseline

## Using PRs to Progress

### When You Hit a PR:
1. Note the conditions (sleep, nutrition, mindset)
2. Check RPE - Was it sustainable?
3. Plan next attempt - When will you try to beat it?
4. Adjust program - Maybe increase working weights

### When PRs Stall:
1. Review volume - Are you doing enough work?
2. Check recovery - Sleep, nutrition, stress?
3. Vary stimulus - Try different rep ranges
4. Be patient - Plateaus are normal

## Motivation from PRs

**PRs prove progress.** On tough days, review your PR history:
- See how far you've come
- Remember what you've overcome
- Visualize future PRs
- Trust the process

title: Compliance Score Explained
content:
# Compliance Score Explained

Your **Compliance Score** measures how consistently you complete your prescribed workouts. It's one of the strongest predictors of rehabilitation success.

## What is Compliance?

**Compliance** = (Sessions Completed ÷ Sessions Prescribed) × 100

**Example:**
- Prescribed: 12 sessions in 4 weeks
- Completed: 10 sessions
- Compliance: (10 ÷ 12) × 100 = **83.3%**

## Why Compliance Matters

Research shows:
- **80%+ compliance**: Excellent outcomes expected
- **60-79% compliance**: Good outcomes likely
- **Below 60%**: Outcomes compromised

**Bottom line**: Showing up is half the battle. Consistency beats intensity.

## Compliance Tiers

### Elite (90-100%)
- 🏆 Nearly perfect adherence
- Misses only 1 session per 10
- Exceptional outcomes expected

### Excellent (80-89%)
- ⭐ Very strong adherence
- Misses ~1 session per month
- Great outcomes expected

### Good (70-79%)
- ✅ Solid adherence
- Some missed sessions, but consistent
- Positive outcomes likely

### Fair (60-69%)
- ⚠️ Inconsistent adherence
- Progress will be slower
- Outcomes uncertain

### Poor (Below 60%)
- ❌ Low adherence
- Minimal progress expected
- Re-evaluate commitment or barriers

## How Compliance is Tracked

Modus calculates compliance:

### Daily
- Did you complete today's session?
- Updates immediately after completion

### Weekly
- Sessions this week ÷ Prescribed this week
- Rolling 7-day window

### Monthly
- Sessions this month ÷ Prescribed this month
- Shows longer-term trends

### Overall (Program)
- Total completed ÷ Total prescribed
- Your cumulative adherence

## Viewing Your Compliance

Check your score in:
- **Analytics tab** - Main compliance widget
- **Progress charts** - Compliance over time
- **Profile page** - Overall score badge
- **Session summary** - After each workout

## What Hurts Compliance?

Common barriers:
- ⏰ **Time constraints** - Work, family demands
- 🏋️ **Gym access** - Equipment or location issues
- 😞 **Motivation** - Hard to stay consistent
- 😣 **Pain/setbacks** - Injury flares
- ❓ **Unclear instructions** - Not sure what to do

## Improving Compliance

### Practical Strategies

1. **Schedule it** - Block time like any appointment
2. **Prepare equipment** - Gym bag ready to go
3. **Start small** - 20 minutes is better than zero
4. **Track progress** - Seeing improvement motivates
5. **Remove friction** - Home workouts if gym is barrier

### Mental Strategies

1. **Focus on identity** - "I'm someone who shows up"
2. **Two-day rule** - Never miss two sessions in a row
3. **Celebrate consistency** - Not just PRs
4. **Visual cues** - Put gym clothes where you'll see them
5. **Accountability** - Share goals with therapist/friend

## Acceptable Reasons to Miss

Some misses are healthy:
- ✅ **Illness** - Rest when sick
- ✅ **Injury flare** - Listen to your body
- ✅ **Life events** - Family emergencies, etc.
- ✅ **Scheduled deload** - Planned recovery weeks

**Key**: Communicate with your therapist and resume quickly.

## Unacceptable Patterns

Red flags:
- ❌ **Frequent last-minute cancellations** - Shows low priority
- ❌ **No communication** - Therapist can't help what they don't know
- ❌ **Repeated excuses** - Indicates barriers need addressing
- ❌ **Long gaps** - Deconditioning and lost progress

## Communicating with Your Therapist

If compliance drops:

1. **Be honest** - Explain real barriers
2. **Problem-solve together** - Adjust frequency, location, or exercises
3. **Recommit** - Set realistic goals going forward
4. **Check in regularly** - Accountability helps

## Compliance vs. Perfection

**Important**: 100% is NOT required for success.

- Life happens - 80% is excellent
- Quality matters - One focused session beats two rushed ones
- Long-term consistency - Better than short-term perfection

## Gamification Features

### Streaks
- **Current streak** - Consecutive sessions completed
- **Longest streak** - Your record
- **Streak milestones** - 7, 14, 30, 90 days

### Badges
- **80% Club** - Month with 80%+ compliance
- **Perfect Week** - All sessions completed
- **Comeback Kid** - Returned after a lapse

### Leaderboards (Optional)
- Compare with other patients (anonymized)
- Friendly competition can motivate

## The Bottom Line

**Compliance is the X-factor.** You can have:
- The perfect program
- The best therapist
- Top equipment

...but without consistency, results won't come.

**Show up. Log your work. Repeat.**

That's the formula for rehabilitation success.
